notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s how to think about your quarterly estimated taxes using your actual numbers, without this being tax advice.

From your data (June 1, 2025 – May 31, 2026, as of June 5, 2026):

Consulting income: $13,960 (all in May 2026)
Tax reserve (Ally Tax Reserve Savings): $18,500
2026 estimated tax paid so far: $4,200 on April 15, 2026
Unknowns: W‑2 withholding, prior‑year credits, your actual federal/state effective tax rates

I’ll walk through concepts and example math so you can plug in your real tax rates or confirm with a tax pro.

1. Safe harbor basics (why they matter)

Safe harbor rules are basically how you avoid underpayment penalties, even if your estimates aren’t perfect. Common federal safe‑harbor rules (simplified):

You generally avoid penalties if by year‑end you’ve paid in at least:

90% of your current‑year total tax, or
100% of last year’s total tax (110% if your AGI was high), via:
W‑2 withholding
Estimated payments
Amounts applied from last year’s refund

Because Monarch doesn’t show last year’s total tax or your W‑2 withholding, you’d typically:

Look at the “total tax” line on last year’s 1040 (and state return), and
See how much has already been or will be withheld from any W‑2 income this year.
2. Rough example using your consulting income

Let’s pretend:

Your combined marginal tax rate (federal + state + self‑employment) on this consulting income is about 30%
(You’d swap in your own rate based on last year’s return or a calculator.)

On $13,960 of consulting income:

Estimated total tax on that income ≈
$13,960 × 30% ≈ $4,188

You’ve already:

Paid $4,200 as an estimated payment in April 2026
Set aside $18,500 in your tax reserve

So in this simplified example:

Your April 15 payment alone roughly covers the tax on the consulting income recorded so far.
Your tax reserve is much larger than the tax implied by this one month of consulting income, which suggests:
Either you expect much more consulting income later in 2026, or
The reserve also covers W‑2 income taxes, prior‑year balances, or just a conservative buffer.
3. How to think about your next estimated payments

Here’s a structured way to approach the next quarters:

Estimate full‑year consulting income

If $13,960 was a one‑off month, maybe you expect, say, $X total for 2026.
If it’s recurring, you might annualize (for example, $13,960 × 6 more months, etc.).
Your own forecast matters a lot here.

Apply an estimated tax rate

Use last year’s return or an online calculator to estimate your combined rate on self‑employment income (including self‑employment tax).
Example: If you expect $40,000 consulting income and use 30%:
Estimated tax on consulting = $40,000 × 30% = $12,000

Layer in W‑2 withholding and other income

If you have W‑2 income, your withholding may already cover part (or all) of your safe‑harbor requirement.
Compare:
Last year’s total tax vs. this year’s expected withholding + estimates
Or this year’s expected total tax vs. this year’s expected withholding + estimates

Compare to what you’ve already paid

Example: If your target for the year is $12,000 of total tax on consulting income and you’ve already paid $4,200:
Remaining target ≈ $7,800
You can split that across the remaining estimated payment dates (for example, June 15, Sept 15, Jan 15) in a way that feels manageable.

Use your tax reserve as a buffer

With $18,500 in your tax reserve and only $4,200 used so far, you have room to:
Make additional estimates as income comes in
Adjust up or down as your actual consulting income deviates from your forecast
